title: Get started with rsgl
text: use examples tutorial, create -memory DuckDB database load two tables, cars trees. Let’s query view sample data:
code: library(rsgl) #>  #> Attaching package: 'rsgl' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets': #>  #>     cars, trees library(duckdb) #> Loading required package: DBI  con <- dbConnect(duckdb()) dbWriteTable(con, "cars", cars) dbWriteTable(con, "trees", trees) dbGetQuery(con, "   select *   from cars   limit 5 ") #>   car_id horsepower miles_per_gallon origin year #> 1      1        130               18    USA 1970 #> 2      2        165               15    USA 1970 #> 3      3        150               18    USA 1970 #> 4      4        150               16    USA 1970 #> 5      5        140               17    USA 1970 dbGetQuery(con, "   select *   from trees   limit 5 ") #>   tree_id  age circumference #> 1       1  118            30 #> 2       1  484            58 #> 3       1  664            87 #> 4       1 1004           115 #> 5       1 1231           120

title: Get started with rsgl
text: primary interface rsgl dbGetPlot function, takes DBI database connection SGL statement returns ggplot2 plot. Although examples tutorial use connection DuckDB database, dbGetPlot accept DBI connection.

title: Get started with rsgl
text: keyword precedes data source specification, often name table database. , specify cars table data source.  similar usage keyword SQL, except single data source allowed (.e., comma-separated list table names valid). data multiple sources pre-processing data necessary, SQL subquery can provided:
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using points ") #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from (     select *     from cars     where origin = 'Japan'   )   using points ")

title: Get started with rsgl
text: using keyword precedes name geometric object(s) represent data. Following ggplot2 terminology, geometric objects referred geoms. previous examples demonstrated representing data point geoms.

title: Get started with rsgl
text: visualize keyword precedes aesthetic--column mapping, maps perceivable traits geoms data source columns. prior examples mapped x y positions point geoms data source columns. However, aesthetics may non-positional, shown . visualize keyword closely resembles select keyword within SQL.  addition mapping aesthetics columns, aesthetics can mapped expressions include transformations aggregations.
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y,     origin as color   from cars   using points ") #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).

title: Get started with rsgl
text: SGL supports column-level transformations aggregations, shown binning transformation combined count aggregation produce histogram miles_per_gallon.  see , similarly SQL, SGL group clause aggregation groupings specified. Although SQL supports column-level transformation, grouping, aggregation, desirable SGL provide additional support operations. Statistical graphics often require operations binning natively supported SQL. Additionally, SGL’s column-level transformations aggregations performed scaling, easily replicated using SQL. example feature, binning counting applied log scaling, resulting log-scaled histogram.
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     bin(miles_per_gallon) as x,     count(*) as y   from cars   group by     bin(miles_per_gallon)   using bars ") #> Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_bar()`). dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     bin(miles_per_gallon) as x,     count(*) as y   from cars   group by     bin(miles_per_gallon)   using bars   scale by     log(x) ") #> Warning: Removed 1 row containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_bar()`).

title: Get started with rsgl
text: SGL, geom classified individual represents record (transformation aggregation) distinct geometric object. Alternatively, geom classified collective represents multiple records one geometric object. example, points lines individual collective geoms, respectively, shown data represented using .   collective geoms, collection records represent object determined automatically using reasonable defaults. behavior can overridden providing explicit collections collect clause. collect clause similar group clause, except rather defining groups aggregate , collect clause defines collections records represented one object. see example default collection ideal, followed preferrable explicit collection.
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     year as x,     avg(miles_per_gallon) as y   from cars   group by     year   using points ") #> Warning: Removed 3 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     year as x,     avg(miles_per_gallon) as y   from cars   group by     year   using line ") #> Warning: Removed 3 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_line()`). dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     age as x,     circumference as y   from trees   using line ") dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     age as x,     circumference as y   from trees   collect by     tree_id   using lines ")

title: Get started with rsgl
text: Geom qualifiers modify geoms positionally represent data, specified keywords precede geom names within using clause. Geom qualifiers can largely classified two groups, statistical qualifiers collision qualifiers. Statistical qualifiers modify positional representation via statistical transformation, linear regression:  Collision qualifiers specify positional adjustments relevant overlapping objects. see example using jittered qualifier add small amount random variation overlapping points discernible. Without jittered qualifier:  jittered qualifier:
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     age as x,     circumference as y   from trees   using regression line ") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     origin as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using points ") #> Warning: Removed 8 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     origin as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using jittered points ") #> Warning: Removed 8 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).

title: Get started with rsgl
text: graphics previous sections contain single layer geometric objects. However, common use multiple layers objects represent data single graphic. SGL, multiple layers can combined using layer operator. example, can layer regression line scatterplot:  Layers often share data source aesthetic mapping. reduce verbosity cases, layer operator can applied directly geom expressions:  Layers may different data sources aesthetic mappings. However, graphic single scale aesthetic, regardless number layers. result, given aesthetic must mapped consistent type across layers present, e.g. aesthetic mapped numerical type one layer categorical type another.
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using points    layer    visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using regression line ") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range #> (`stat_smooth()`). #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using (     points     layer     regression line   ) ") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range #> (`stat_smooth()`). #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).

title: Get started with rsgl
text: mapped aesthetic scale determines data values mapped corresponding visual property. Scales determined implicitly default, can explicitly specified within scale clause. specify log scale x y aesthetics, overriding default linear scaling numerical mappings:  Scaling performed prior column-level transformations aggregations. Additionally, scaling performed prior positional modifications specified geom qualifiers, e.g., regression calculation performed log-scaling x y aesthetics. Scaling functions log applied aesthetic names rather column names modifications aesthetic scales rather actual data values. distinction demonstrated , log function instead applied actual data values SQL subquery:
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using (     points     layer     regression line   )   scale by     log(x), log(y) ") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range #> (`stat_smooth()`). #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     log_hp as x,     log_mpg as y   from (     select       log(horsepower) as log_hp,       log(miles_per_gallon) as log_mpg     from cars   )   using (     points     layer     regression line   ) ") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing non-finite outside the scale range #> (`stat_smooth()`). #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).

title: Get started with rsgl
text: graphics prior examples use Cartesian coordinates, alternative coordinate systems valid. SGL, coordinate system inferred positional aesthetics visualize clause. example, x y aesthetics imply Cartesian coordinates, whereas theta r imply polar coordinates. examples display information Cartesian polar coordinates. grammar graphics (SGL based ), pie charts stacked bar charts polar coordinate system. SGL, bar geom stacked default.   Since SGL statement may multiple layers, may also multiple visualize clauses, positional aesthetic mappings. Since graphic single coordinate system, positional aesthetics referenced across layers must consistent, e.g. one layer reference x y aesthetics another references theta r.
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     count(*) as y,     origin as color   from cars   group by     origin   using bars ") dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     count(*) as theta,     origin as color   from cars   group by     origin   using bars ")

title: Get started with rsgl
text: Faceting generates small multiples panel represents different partition source data. Partitioning determined unique values expressions specified facet clause. Faceting single expression generates horizontal panels default, can modified orientation keyword:   Two facet expressions may specified, case one expression represented horizontally represented vertically:  SGL, facets graphic-level property, meaning SGL statement one facet clause, layer faceted accordingly.
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using points   facet by     origin ") #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using points   facet by     origin vertically ") #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`). dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from (     select       *,       case         when year < 1977         then '< 1977'         else '>= 1977'       end as 'era'     from cars   )   using points   facet by     era,     origin ") #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).

title: Get started with rsgl
text: Titles aesthetic scales automatically determined aesthetic mappings. However, can overridden providing explicit titles title clause:
code: dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     horsepower as x,     miles_per_gallon as y   from cars   using points   title     x as 'Horsepower',     y as 'Miles Per Gallon' ") #> Warning: Removed 14 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).

what: rsgl — An R Implementation of the SGL Graphics Language
title: rsgl — An R Implementation of the SGL Graphics Language
text: rsgl implements SGL graphics language use within R. SGL graphics language designed look feel like SQL, based grammar graphics.

title: rsgl — An R Implementation of the SGL Graphics Language
text: rsgl yet available CRAN can installed GitHub :
code: # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("sgl-projects/rsgl")

title: rsgl — An R Implementation of the SGL Graphics Language
text: dbGetPlot primary interface rsgl. takes DBI database connection SGL statement returns ggplot2 plot. following example demonstrates creating DBI connection -memory DuckDB database, loading data, generating scatterplot SGL statement.
code: library(duckdb) #> Warning: package 'duckdb' was built under R version 4.5.2 #> Loading required package: DBI #> Warning: package 'DBI' was built under R version 4.5.2 library(rsgl)  con <- dbConnect(duckdb()) dbWriteTable(con, "cars", mtcars)  dbGetPlot(con, "     visualize         hp as x,         mpg as y     from cars     using points ")

title: Cars dataset — cars
text: sample dataset used throughout rsgl documentation examples containing attributes collection cars. Loading rsgl masks datasets::cars; refer built-version datasets::cars need .

title: Cars dataset — cars
code: cars

title: Cars dataset — cars
text: data frame 406 rows 5 variables: car_id Integer identifier car. horsepower Engine horsepower. miles_per_gallon Fuel economy miles per US gallon. origin Country origin (USA, Europe, Japan). year Model year (1970–1982).

what: Generate a plot from a SGL statement — dbGetPlot
title: Generate a plot from a SGL statement — dbGetPlot
text: dbGetPlot takes DBI connection SGL statement returns corresponding plot.

title: Generate a plot from a SGL statement — dbGetPlot
code: dbGetPlot(con, sgl_stmt)

title: Generate a plot from a SGL statement — dbGetPlot
text: con DBI connection sgl_stmt SGL statement (string)

title: Generate a plot from a SGL statement — dbGetPlot
text: plot defined SGL statement (ggplot2 plot object)

title: Generate a plot from a SGL statement — dbGetPlot
code: library(duckdb) #> Loading required package: DBI con <- dbConnect(duckdb()) dbWriteTable(con, "cars", mtcars) p <- dbGetPlot(con, "   visualize     hp as x,     mpg as y   from cars   using points ") print(p)

title: Trees dataset — trees
text: dataset used throughout rsgl documentation examples containing repeated measurements tree growth time. Loading rsgl masks datasets::trees; refer built-version datasets::trees need .

title: Trees dataset — trees
code: trees

title: Trees dataset — trees
text: data frame 35 rows 3 variables: tree_id Integer identifier tree. age Age tree (days) time measurement. circumference Trunk circumference time measurement.

what: Get SGL type classificatons for columns in a table — type_classifications
title: Get SGL type classificatons for columns in a table — type_classifications
text: type_classifications takes DBI connection table name returns SGL type classifications (numerical, categorical, temporal) table's columns.

title: Get SGL type classificatons for columns in a table — type_classifications
code: type_classifications(con, table_name)

title: Get SGL type classificatons for columns in a table — type_classifications
text: con DBI connection table_name name table

title: Get SGL type classificatons for columns in a table — type_classifications
text: dataframe listing SGL type classification column.

title: Get SGL type classificatons for columns in a table — type_classifications
code: library(duckdb) con <- dbConnect(duckdb()) dbWriteTable(con, "iris", iris) type_classifications(con, "iris") #>    column_name column_class #> 1 Sepal.Length    numerical #> 2  Sepal.Width    numerical #> 3 Petal.Length    numerical #> 4  Petal.Width    numerical #> 5      Species  categorical
